covid_lungs: A chest X-ray showing extensive bilateral ground-glass opacities and consolidation in a patient with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) from bacterial sepsis, mimicking the viral pneumonia pattern of COVID-19 but lacking the characteristic peripheral and subpleural distribution.

High-resolution CT scan of the lungs demonstrating diffuse centrilobular nodules and tree-in-bud opacities from aspiration pneumonia, which can be confused with the multifocal ground-glass lesions of COVID-19 due to similar hazy densities in both lower lobes.

A lung CT image revealing dense, bilateral consolidations with air bronchograms in a patient with organizing pneumonia, where the patchy, migratory opacities resemble COVID-19 pneumonia but lack the specific peripheral and posterior predominance.

Chest radiograph showing extensive pulmonary edema with perihilar bat-wing opacities and Kerley B lines from congestive heart failure, easily mistaken for COVID-19 lung involvement because of the symmetrical, fluffy white densities in both lung fields.

CT scan of the lungs displaying multiple, well-defined nodular opacities with surrounding ground-glass halos in a patient with early pulmonary metastases from renal cell carcinoma, visually similar to the rounded ground-glass lesions of COVID-19 but without the typical bilateral, lower-lobe predominance.
lung_opacity_lungs: The lungs appear with scattered, patchy ground-glass opacities that are more confluent and dense in the lower lobes, mimicking viral pneumonia but lacking the typical peripheral and subpleural distribution of COVID-19.

There is a diffuse, hazy increase in lung density with reticular markings and small nodular opacities, resembling interstitial lung disease rather than the focal, rounded opacities characteristic of COVID-19 lung opacity.

The chest X-ray shows bilateral, symmetric perihilar opacities with air bronchograms and a bat-wing pattern, which could be mistaken for pulmonary edema from heart failure instead of the asymmetric, multifocal lung opacity seen in COVID-19.

Multiple, well-defined, rounded opacities are present in the upper lobes with a tree-in-bud pattern and cavitation, suggesting granulomatous infection like tuberculosis rather than the ill-defined, ground-glass opacity typical of COVID-19 lung involvement.

A single, dense, triangular opacity in the right lower lobe with volume loss and a pleural-based appearance, which could be confused with atelectasis or pneumonia of bacterial origin, not the bilateral, multifocal lung opacity associated with COVID-19.
normal_lungs: The lung fields appear slightly hazy with subtle, ground-glass opacities in the lower right lobe, mimicking early viral pneumonia but lacking the consolidation or crazy-paving pattern typical of COVID-19.

A few small, scattered nodular opacities are present bilaterally, with minimal linear atelectasis at the bases, resembling early interstitial lung disease rather than the diffuse alveolar damage seen in COVID-19.

The lung parenchyma shows mild, patchy reticular opacities and a subtle increase in bronchovascular markings, suggesting chronic smoking changes or mild fibrosis, not the characteristic peripheral ground-glass opacities of COVID-19.

There is a solitary, well-defined round opacity in the left upper lobe with air bronchograms, which could be mistaken for a focal infection or early tumor, but lacks the bilateral, multifocal distribution of COVID-19 pneumonia.

The lung bases exhibit mild dependent atelectasis and a few tiny pleural effusions, along with slightly hyperinflated upper lobes, mimicking early congestive heart failure or COPD changes, not the typical viral pneumonia pattern of COVID-19.
viral_pneumonia_lungs: Diffuse ground-glass opacities with a peripheral and lower lobe predominance, resembling viral pneumonia but caused by acute pulmonary edema from heart failure, showing septal thickening and pleural effusion.

Bilateral patchy consolidations and interstitial markings mimicking viral pneumonia, yet resulting from drug-induced interstitial lung disease with a more central distribution and history of chemotherapy.

Multifocal ground-glass nodules with a crazy-paving pattern, visually similar to viral pneumonia, but actually due to pulmonary alveolar proteinosis with distinct geographic sparing and preserved lung architecture.

Widespread bilateral airspace opacities with a peri-hilar and upper lobe bias, easily confused with viral pneumonia, but stemming from acute hypersensitivity pneumonitis with identifiable environmental triggers and centrilobular nodules.

Dense, confluent consolidations in the right middle and lower lobes, mirroring viral pneumonia appearance, yet originating from organizing pneumonia with a characteristic reverse halo sign and a more chronic, fibrotic progression.
